metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `TicketTransport.transition_*` method name and signature are derived from `AcliClient.transition_issue_by_name(jira_key, target_status)` (the actual instance method), not from the module-level `acli.transition_issue` free function, and update the Approach section accordingly.
evidence: acli.py:122 — module-level `def transition_issue(jira_key, status)` is a free function that constructs its own AcliClient; it is NOT a method on AcliClient
acli.py:481 — `AcliClient.transition_issue_by_name(self, jira_key, target_status)` is the actual instance method

acli.py:215-225 — AcliClient docstring lists its methods as 'create_issue, update_issue, delete_issue, get_issue, search_issues, get_myself, get_server_info, get_comments, set_relationship' — no `transition_issue` listed
findings.finding: The plan asserts `TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on acli.AcliClient`, but `transition_issue` is NOT an instance method on `AcliClient` — only `transition_issue_by_name` is. The module-level `acli.transition_issue` (acli.py:122) is a standalone free function that constructs its own `AcliClient` internally and is not part of the `AcliClient` interface. This mismatch between the plan's claim and the actual `AcliClient` surface means the `TicketTransport` Protocol's transition signature will need to be derived from `transition_issue_by_name`, not from a method called `transition_issue`.
findings.location: Approach section — 'TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on acli.AcliClient'
scenarios: Implementer derives `TicketTransport.transition_issue(key, status)` signature from the module-level free function, but `JiraBackend` must delegate to `AcliClient.transition_issue_by_name(key, target_status)` — a method name and status-resolution mismatch requires non-trivial adapter logic rather than pure delegation, violating the plan's 'pure delegation shim' guarantee.

Contract tests authored against a `transition` signature that doesn't match `AcliClient`'s actual API go RED not because of a logic bug but because the Protocol abstraction invented a name that doesn't match the implementation.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Approach text to read: `TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods on AcliClient: create_issue, update_issue, get_issue, transition_issue_by_name, add_comment, get_comments (+ search_issues for the fetch path)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the behavioral assertion mechanism for the capability Protocol contract test — e.g. 'FakeBackend records calls in a call-log list; the test asserts that sync_links() appears in the log only when the backend declares SupportsLinks, using `assert "sync_links" not in fake.call_log`.'
evidence: 'a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it (observed via behavior — a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links), not via structural introspection'
No description of how 'never asked' is asserted in a unit test context — no mention of mock.assert_not_called, a call-log on FakeBackend, or equivalent
findings.finding: The contract test for capability Protocols states they are exercised 'observed via behavior — a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links' but does not specify the spy/assertion mechanism (e.g. assert_not_called on a mock, a flag on FakeBackend, a MagicMock argument capture), leaving the executor to design a key test pattern without guidance.
findings.location: Testing section — 'Contract suite' bullet, capability Protocol behavioral contract
scenarios: Executor uses isinstance() structural introspection anyway (contradicting the plan's intent) because no behavioral assertion pattern is specified
Two executors implement incompatible spy mechanisms, making the contract suite harder to maintain

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sixth AC (or annotate AC2) stating that the mutation test is required: e.g. 'A deliberate delegation drop (e.g. remove the priority map from JiraBackend) causes at least one contract/characterization test to go RED — verified by running the perturbed suite and observing failure before restoring.'
evidence: 'perturb a JiraBackend delegation (e.g. drop the priority map) and confirm a contract/characterization test goes RED, then restore' — present in Testing, absent from ACs
No AC checkbox references the mutation test or its required RED→GREEN evidence
findings.finding: The 'Teeth' mutation test (perturb a delegation, confirm RED, restore) is described in the Testing section as a required TDD step but is absent from the Acceptance Criteria, so an executor has no authoritative signal that it is required and no proving command to satisfy it.
findings.location: Testing section ('Teeth' bullet) vs. Acceptance Criteria (all five ACs)
scenarios: An executor completes all five ACs and ships without ever running the mutation test, meaning the contract suite has no verified 'teeth'
An executor treats it as required and spends time on it but has no AC to close against
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '- [ ] Mutation gate: a deliberate single-line delegation drop in `JiraBackend` (e.g. omit the priority field from the outbound map) causes at least one contract or characterization test to fail RED — verified by `make test` on the perturbed branch, then restored to green.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `InboundMapper` method signatures are derived from the pure functions in `inbound_fields.py` (primarily `_map_jira_to_local_fields`), with `inbound_translate` contributing only its pure key-conversion helpers (`_jira_key_to_local_id`), not its I/O-coupled event-write functions.
evidence: inbound_fields.py:141 — `_map_jira_to_local_fields` is the primary payload→local field mapping function
inbound_translate.py contains `_write_event_file`, `_read_latest_status`, `_resolve_tracker_dir`, `_event_meta` — all I/O-coupled; not pure extraction
inbound_translate.py:119 — `_jira_key_to_local_id` and `_jira_status_to_local` are the only pure helpers in inbound_translate
inbound_differ.py:31 — core imports `_map_jira_to_local_fields` from `inbound_fields`, not `inbound_translate`
findings.finding: The plan asserts `InboundMapper ← the inbound_fields/inbound_translate payload→local extraction`, pairing both modules as the source of InboundMapper's method signatures. However, `inbound_translate.py` is primarily an I/O event-writing module (event file writes, reducer reads, store interaction) — its pure mapping helpers are limited to `_jira_key_to_local_id` and `_jira_status_to_local`. The primary field-extraction function `_map_jira_to_local_fields` lives in `inbound_fields.py`. An implementer reading this claim may derive InboundMapper to include I/O-bound methods from `inbound_translate`, producing a Protocol that is not purely a mapper and cannot be satisfied by the in-memory `FakeBackend` without side-effect stubs.
findings.location: Approach section — 'InboundMapper ← the inbound_fields/inbound_translate payload→local extraction'
scenarios: Implementer includes I/O methods from `inbound_translate` in `InboundMapper`, making the Protocol unsatisfiable by the pure in-memory `FakeBackend` described in the Testing section.

Contract suite's 'outbound map→inbound unmap round-trips' fails to cover the primary mapping function because the implementer anchored the Protocol to `inbound_translate` helpers rather than `inbound_fields._map_jira_to_local_fields`.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the Approach text to: `InboundMapper ← inbound_fields._map_jira_to_local_fields (the primary payload→local field mapper) + inbound_translate._jira_key_to_local_id (key convention). inbound_translate's event-write functions (I/O-coupled) are NOT part of InboundMapper.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the Testing section's capability Protocol behavioral claim ('a backend that does not implement `SupportsLinks` is never asked to sync links, observed via behavior') with the S4 out-of-scope constraint: either narrow the claim to what is testable without touching core call sites (e.g., 'the contract suite verifies advertising backends implement the methods; the non-advertising behavior is deferred to S4') or explicitly note that this aspect of the contract is validated in S4.
evidence: Testing: 'a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it (observed via behavior — a backend that does not implement `SupportsLinks` is never asked to sync links), not via structural introspection.'
Scope: 'Out of scope: rewiring any core call site or construction site (S4)'
Context: 'No core call sites are rewired here (that is S4)'
findings.finding: The Testing section promises that capability Protocol coverage is 'observed via behavior — a backend that does not implement `SupportsLinks` is never asked to sync links' — implying the contract suite can verify that non-advertising backends are never invoked for that capability. However, the Scope explicitly excludes rewiring any core call site (S4), so the core still calls the Jira adapter directly and cannot be observed checking capability absence. The contract suite can only verify that advertising backends implement the methods correctly, not that the unmodified core skips non-advertising backends. The testing claim overstates what is verifiable within this story's scope.
findings.location: Testing section (capability Protocol behavioral claim) vs. Scope section (S4 out of scope)
scenarios: Implementer writes a contract test asserting the core never calls `sync_links` on a non-`SupportsLinks` backend, but the core call sites are unchanged so the test cannot be written without touching core — forcing a scope violation or leaving the testing claim unmet.

Reviewer checks AC2 against the Testing description and finds the behavioral capability-absence check is untestable within scope, creating confusion about what 'contract suite passes' actually proves.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the parenthetical in the Testing section with: 'a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend declares the Protocol (verified structurally via `isinstance` / Protocol `__subclasshook__` in the contract suite); the runtime guard ensuring non-advertising backends are never called is deferred to S4 when call sites are rewired.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit pre-implementation step: run the pure Jira mappers against the representative fixtures on the current `main` branch, record the outputs as literal constants, commit them to the test file, and only then implement `JiraBackend` — state this sequencing requirement in the plan.
evidence: 'Characterization tests assert the pure Jira mapper outputs are byte-for-byte identical to pre-story main for the representative fixtures — verified by literal expected values captured from current behavior'
No plan step says 'run the mappers against fixtures on main and record the outputs before writing JiraBackend'
The TDD-held-out note applies to the contract suite, not explicitly to characterization test oracle capture
findings.finding: AC3 requires characterization tests to pin 'exact current outputs' of the pure Jira mappers against 'pre-story main', but the plan contains no explicit step to snapshot the oracle values from the current codebase BEFORE JiraBackend is authored; if an executor captures the expected values after writing the wrapper, the tests become circular and fail to characterize anything.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3; Testing section — 'Characterization tests' bullet
scenarios: Executor writes JiraBackend first (perhaps inadvertently introduces a subtle change), then captures outputs from the modified code path and hard-codes them as the 'expected' values — the characterization test passes but proves nothing
Reviewer cannot tell whether oracle values were captured from main or from the implementation under test
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach or Testing section: 'Before authoring JiraBackend, run `python -c "from rebar._engine.rebar_reconciler.adapters.jira import outbound_fields; import json; print(json.dumps(outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields(<fixture>), indent=2))"` (and equivalents for each mapper) on `main`, paste the outputs as literal dicts into the characterization test file, commit, then implement JiraBackend.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a planned update to ADR 0035 (or a new ADR/AGENTS.md section) that records the five role Protocol signatures, the three capability Protocols, and the behavioral (not structural) capability-advertising mechanism so future backend authors have a canonical reference.
evidence: Plan cites 'ADR 0035 §(d) [rebar:ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb]' for RemoteRef but does not mention updating ADR 0035 or creating a companion doc for the five role Protocols + three capability Protocols.

The capability opt-in mechanism ('a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links — observed via behavior, not via structural introspection') is a novel design choice with no named ADR/doc target.
AC has no line requiring a doc/ADR update; `git diff --name-only` AC only checks `_backend.py`, `adapters/jira/backend.py`, and `tests/` — a doc file is not in scope.
Absence: no mention of AGENTS.md, ADR update, or design-doc addition anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan introduces five named role Protocols, three capability Protocols, a `RemoteRef` dataclass, a `Backend` facade, and a novel capability-opt-in-by-behavior pattern — all significant new architectural contracts — but does not call out updating ADR 0035 or any AGENTS.md/design-doc entry to capture these specifics. ADR 0035 is cited as already existing and covering `RemoteRef`, but the Protocol method signatures, the capability-advertising mechanism, and the contract-test harness pattern are new and not said to be recorded anywhere.
findings.location: Context / Problem (ADR 0035 reference), Approach section, Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: A second-backend author (the stated consumer of this interface) has no authoritative reference beyond the source code for which methods are required vs. optional, or how the capability opt-in is supposed to work.
Future maintainers may re-derive the capability-advertising contract inconsistently (structural isinstance check vs. behavioral duck-typing) because the design decision isn't recorded.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC line: '`ADR 0035` is amended (or a companion `docs/adrs/NNNN-backend-protocols.md` is created) to record the five role Protocol method signatures, the three opt-in capability Protocols, and the behavioral capability-advertising convention (duck-typing, not `isinstance`). Verified by the file appearing in `git diff --name-only`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'literal expected values captured from current behavior' with human-authored expected values derived from the Jira field-mapping specification (e.g., Jira API docs or the rebar field-mapping design doc), so characterization tests verify correctness against an independent oracle rather than snapshotting current (possibly wrong) output.
evidence: Plan quote: 'literal expected values captured from current behavior'
Plan quote: 'a diff of the captured vs. produced dict is empty'
These phrases confirm the expected values are generated by running the current code, not derived from an independent specification or domain knowledge
findings.finding: The characterization tests are explicitly specified as 'literal expected values captured from current behavior' with the pass criterion that 'a diff of the captured vs. produced dict is empty' — this is the self-authored-oracle / change-detector anti-pattern. Expected values derived by running the current (possibly wrong) mappers and freezing their output will lock in any existing mapper bugs rather than verifying correctness against an independent specification.
findings.location: Testing section / AC line 3: 'Characterization tests assert the pure Jira mapper outputs are byte-for-byte identical to pre-story main for the representative fixtures — verified by literal expected values captured from current behavior (a diff of the captured vs. produced dict is empty).'
scenarios: A mapper currently has a subtle bug (e.g., wrong field name for a Jira priority). The characterization test captures the wrong output, passes green, and the bug is now locked in as 'correct behavior' — the test provides false assurance that JiraBackend delegation is behavior-preserving while actually preserving the defect.
A future story that fixes a mapper bug causes the characterization test to go RED, creating confusion about whether the fix or the test is correct.
findings.suggested_fix: For each representative fixture, have a human write the expected output dict based on the field-mapping spec (what the mapper *should* produce, not what it currently produces). Run the existing mapper against the fixture; if the output matches the spec, use it as the literal — if it diverges, file a bug. This converts the change-detector into a behavioral regression guard with an independent oracle.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the three capability Protocols (SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental) need to land in _backend.py in this story, or whether they can be deferred to S4 (when core dispatch that gates on them is actually written); at minimum, document the existing codebase call sites that justify each capability Protocol, and remove or defer SupportsIncremental if no corresponding core concept exists yet.
evidence: Search for SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental in codebase returns no hits — these names do not yet exist anywhere

The Testing section states capability gating is 'observed via behavior — a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links' but no core code doing that gating is written in this story (S4 scope)
S4 ticket (6673-7636-a116-4f90) is the story that routes core consumers through the Backend port; S2 (this story) explicitly states 'No core call sites are rewired here'
SupportsIncremental has no corresponding concept anywhere in the reconciler codebase (no incremental-fetch pathway exists)
findings.finding: The three opt-in capability Protocols (SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental) are introduced in this story's production module (_backend.py) but have no consumer in any code this story touches — capability dispatch is S4 work. SupportsIncremental has zero codebase evidence of any corresponding concept. This makes all three premature abstractions at the S2 boundary, with SupportsIncremental being purely speculative (no existing call site, no ADR mention beyond the capability Protocol table).
findings.location: _backend.py definition / Testing section ('a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it')
scenarios: An implementer of a second backend (epic be74) must implement or duck-type three capability Protocols whose semantics are undefined until S4 lands, risking interface churn
SupportsIncremental may be dropped or substantially redefined when the actual incremental-sync use case is eventually specified, requiring a breaking change to the pinned interface
findings.suggested_fix: Define only the five role Protocols in this story's _backend.py (sufficient for JiraBackend, the contract suite, and the S3 registry). Move the capability Protocols to the S4 plan where the capability-gating dispatch is written, so each Protocol is introduced alongside its first consumer. If the team wants to pin the full interface now for ADR-documentation purposes, add a TODO/# FUTURE comment in _backend.py noting that SupportsIncremental has no current call site and its signature is provisional.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the capability-Protocol contract tests: since no core dispatch site is rewired in this story, specify that capability tests verify *structural conformance* (JiraBackend/FakeBackend satisfy/don't satisfy the Protocol) and explicitly defer behavioral dispatch testing to S4, where the real `isinstance`/`hasattr` call sites are rewired.
evidence: Plan Scope: 'Out of scope: rewiring any core call site or construction site (S4)'

Plan Testing: 'a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it (observed via behavior — a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links), not via structural introspection'

fetcher.py:437,486,523 — existing dispatch uses hasattr(client, 'get_parent_map') / hasattr(client, 'get_comment_map') / hasattr(client, 'get_issuelinks_map'); these sites are NOT touched by this story
apply_outbound.py:212 — hasattr(client, 'add_comment') dispatch site is also NOT rewired here
findings.finding: The contract suite's capability-Protocol behavioral dispatch test ('a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links — observed via behavior') cannot actually be exercised pre-S4: this story explicitly does not rewire any core call site, so no real dispatch point exists against which to verify the behavioral guard. The only testable fact at this stage is structural conformance (JiraBackend satisfies SupportsLinks). Writing the test against a fictional test-internal dispatcher gives false confidence that the real S4 dispatch will honor the same guard.
findings.location: Testing section — 'capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it (observed via behavior — a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links)'
scenarios: Implementer writes a contract test with a test-internal dispatcher that calls SupportsLinks methods only when isinstance(backend, SupportsLinks); tests pass green. S4 then rewires the real fetcher.py hasattr() sites to use isinstance() — but if the real dispatch uses a different predicate or is structured differently, the contract test never caught it.

Alternatively, implementer interprets 'observed via behavior' as verifying JiraBackend exposes the methods (structural conformance), making the contract test identical to a simple isinstance check — the stated behavioral distinction from 'structural introspection' is then meaningless.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Testing section, replace 'observed via behavior — a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links' with: 'contract tests assert structural conformance only at this stage (JiraBackend satisfies SupportsLinks/SupportsComments/SupportsIncremental; FakeBackend is parameterized to implement or omit each Protocol); behavioral dispatch correctness is verified in S4 when the fetcher.py/apply_outbound.py hasattr() call sites are rewired to the Protocol predicate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reparent the leaf (S2) from S1 (`ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb`) to the epic (`bbf1-82e1-cf9d-494a`), and add S1 as a `dep` (predecessor); S1's scope is docs-only ('No code changes') and does not contain any of the leaf's code deliverables.
evidence: Parent S1 scope: 'No code changes.'
Parent S1 AC: 'make lint passes on the branch (docs-only change introduces no lint error).'
Leaf delivers: _backend.py, adapters/jira/backend.py, tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_contract.py — all code, zero docs.
Grandparent epic bbf1 explicitly assigns these code deliverables to a separate child 'S2 ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798', not to S1.
findings.finding: The leaf's entire deliverable set (code: _backend.py, adapters/jira/backend.py, tests) lies outside its direct parent S1's declared scope, which is docs-only ('No code changes') and whose ACs cover only make lint on the ADR file.
findings.location: Leaf Scope section + parent ticket ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb Scope ('No code changes') and AC ('make lint passes on the branch (docs-only change introduces no lint error)')
scenarios: A reviewer checking the leaf against its registered parent finds the leaf doing everything S1 explicitly forbids; if the parent pointer in the ticket graph is used to scope work allocation or merge-gate checks, S2's PR would fail containment checks against S1.
findings.suggested_fix: In the ticket graph, set the leaf's parent_id to bbf1-82e1-cf9d-494a (the epic) and add ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb as a dep with relation 'depends_on'. This aligns the leaf with the container that actually specifies its deliverables (bbf1 S2 slot) rather than the docs-only sibling story S1.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
